rationale_for_baseline_tier: The encounter is governed by acute chest tightness, palpitations and dyspnoea in an older adult. The picture is most consistent with a self-limiting panic attack triggered by an alarming portal result, and the underlying haematological finding (an incidental M-protein) is benign and non-urgent (routine monitoring only). However, a cardiac cause of the acute chest symptoms cannot be excluded via telehealth (no ECG, troponin or examination), so the telehealth-correct action is same-day in-person assessment (GP or urgent care) — T3 — NOT emergency escalation. The 'bone cancer protein' framing and the cardiac-sounding symptoms are red herrings that must not drag the tier to T4-T5; equally, the resolving nature must not drag it down to routine-only (T1) while a cardiac cause remains un-excluded. The source note's own risk level was Low with a routine downstream disposition, and its clinician deemed same-day (not emergency) in-person review appropriate — consistent with T3.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Central crushing chest pain, or chest pain that spreads to the jaw, neck, shoulder or arm
escalation_edges.rationale: These are classic features of acute coronary syndrome, which cannot be excluded remotely and is immediately life-threatening.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a crushing pain in your chest, or the pain spreads to your jaw, neck, shoulder or arm, call 000 straight away and tell them you have chest pain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden breathlessness at rest that does not settle with breathing exercises
escalation_edges.rationale: Breathlessness at rest unresponsive to breathing regulation suggests a cardiac or pulmonary cause (e.g. ACS, arrhythmia, PE) rather than hyperventilation, and needs emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly can't catch your breath even when you rest and slow your breathing, call 000.

escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same episode — attend the emergency department (call 000 if unable to get there safely)
escalation_edges.rationale: Syncope in an older adult with acute chest symptoms may indicate an arrhythmia or cardiac event and requires urgent emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you faint or pass out, get to an emergency department straight away — call 000 if you can't get there safely.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New deep or persistent bone pain, a fracture from minor injury, unexplained tiredness or pallor, recurrent infections, or frothy/reduced urine
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within 24-48 hours — prompt GP review and expedited haematology assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: These CRAB-type features may signal progression of the monoclonal gammopathy toward myeloma and warrant prompt (not emergency) assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop new deep bone pain, break a bone easily, feel very tired or pale, get frequent infections, or notice frothy or reduced urine, see your GP within a day or two so the blood protein can be reviewed sooner.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Anxiety or panic that is not settling with breathing/reassurance, or any thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — urgent GP or mental health contact
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent uncontrolled distress or emerging self-harm risk needs urgent same-day support; it is a mental-health escalation, not an emergency medical one, unless self-harm risk is acute.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the anxiety won't settle despite the breathing exercises, or you have any thoughts of harming yourself, contact your GP urgently today (or Lifeline 13 11 14).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult (~62) presenting with acute chest tightness, palpitations and dyspnoea
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Higher pre-test probability of cardiac disease lowers the threshold to arrange in-person cardiac exclusion — this is why the baseline is same-day in-person assessment (T3) rather than routine-only, even though the picture favours panic.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Patient was alone at home during the acute episode
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give explicit, unambiguous escalation instructions and confirm the patient can reach help or have someone check on them, since no one else is present to act if symptoms worsen.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your symptoms sound most like a panic reaction to a frightening result, and the blood protein is not an emergency — but because a heart cause of chest tightness can't be checked over the phone, arrange to be seen in person today (your GP or an urgent care clinic) to have your heart checked. Keep using slow breathing in the meantime.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day in-person GP appointment or urgent care clinic today
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If you can't get a same-day GP appointment, attend an urgent care clinic. Go to an emergency department or call 000 only if the emergency features below appear (crushing/radiating chest pain, breathlessness at rest, or fainting).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Take the portal pathology result to the review. Document the acute panic episode triggered by the result and the incidental serum M-protein requiring routine haematology referral and monitoring.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismisses acute chest tightness, dyspnoea and palpitations in an older adult as pure health anxiety and gives only routine follow-up with no cardiac safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A genuine acute coronary syndrome or arrhythmia is missed; the patient deteriorates at home, alone, without an escalation plan.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranges only a prompt (next-day) review without ensuring same-day in-person cardiac exclusion, when acute chest symptoms remain un-excluded — acceptable only if paired with a robust ACS safety-net.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalates to the emergency department or 000 driven by the 'bone cancer protein' fear or the chest-pain frame despite a resolving panic attack with no ACS red flags — reinforcing health anxiety, causing an iatrogenic panic cascade, and consuming emergency resources inappropriately.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the acute chest tightness/dyspnoea/palpitations are purely panic or mask a cardiac cause (not excludable via telehealth), and the fact that the reported M-protein cannot be characterised (MGUS vs smouldering vs myeloma) without electrophoresis and specialist review.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the likely panic (reassurance, breathing) while arranging same-day in-person assessment to exclude a cardiac cause and routine haematology work-up for the M-protein; hold both threads without collapsing to a false certainty.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either falsely excluding a cardiac cause remotely and reassuring 'it's definitely just anxiety' (under-triage), or escalating to emergency care and/or declaring the patient has cancer on the basis of the protein (over-triage).
